name: Between Musa and Dawud
epithet: Joshua, a plague, a hundred years asleep, and a boy with a sling

era.place: From the wilderness to the holy land

title: Between Musa and Dawud: Yusha, Uzair, Talut, and the boy who felled Jalut
meta_description: Day 25 of 29: the bridge from Musa to Dawud. Yusha leading a new generation into the holy land, the people who fled death only to meet it, the man Allah let die a hundred years, and the small believing band that broke Jalut's army through a shepherd boy named Dawud. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets.
keywords: between Musa and Dawud, Yusha ibn Nun, Joshua, Uzair, the hundred years, al-Baqarah 2:259, Talut, Saul, Jalut, Goliath, Dawud and Goliath, how many a small company, Badr, Mufti Menk, stories of the prophets, Buruja

video.title: Stories Of The Prophets-25~Musa, Uzair, Hizqeel, Yushua, Dawud - Part 1

source_note: Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets, episode 25 (the figures between Musa and Dawud: Yusha, Hizqeel, Uzair, Talut, and Jalut). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is Mufti Menk's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: Some days in this series are one life told slowly. Today is different. It is the corridor between two giants, Musa who has just left us and Dawud who is about to arrive, and Mufti Menk walks us down it past a handful of figures most of us have never had named for us: a prophet who led a new generation into the holy land, a people who tried to outrun death, a man Allah let sleep through a whole century, and finally a shepherd boy nobody took seriously.

It looks like a string of loose episodes. It is not. Underneath every one of them is a single question your own faith hangs on, the question of whether Allah can give life back to the dead, and the answer the corridor keeps returning, again and again, in different voices: yes, easily, watch.

sections.h2: Musa is told it is time
paras: When we left Musa, peace be upon him, his people were being walked in circles. They had refused to enter the holy land when he led them to its edge, so Allah sentenced them to forty years of wandering: every morning they set out, and every evening they were back where they began. Harun, peace be upon him, died in those years, and then, in those same years, death came for Musa himself.

Mufti Menk tells the moment from an authentic narration of the Prophet ﷺ, and it is a strange and tender one. When the angel of death came to Musa and told him his time had come, Musa struck him. The angel returned to his Lord: You sent me to a servant who does not want to die. So Allah sent him back with an offer. Put your hand on the back of an ox, Musa; for every hair beneath your palm, another year of life. And Musa asked the only question that matters: and then what? And then, death. In that case, he said, let it come now. The man who would not be slapped into the grave walked into it willingly the instant he understood that no number of years changes the destination.

Mufti Menk turns it on us before we can look away. Someone once asked him about donating their body to science after death, so an organ might give another person a few more years. His point was never a ruling on medicine, but a question for the heart: how many years, he asked back, five, ten? It is the same arithmetic Musa did. If you are not ready to meet Allah now, when exactly will you be ready? Allah even says of some who came after, you will find them clinging to life more than anyone, and you can watch it today in the men who pay to freeze their bodies hoping a future century will thaw them back. We will not outrun the meeting. Far better to prepare for it. Musa, peace be upon him, asked only to live long enough to see the holy land entered; when he saw that was not Allah's decree, he let go without a fight. He was buried, the Prophet ﷺ tells us, near a red sandhill by the roadside, and on the Night Journey he ﷺ passed him there, standing in prayer in his grave.

sections.h2: A new generation, and the sun that waited
paras: After Musa, Allah raised a prophet to finish what the wandering generation would not: Yusha ibn Nun, Joshua, peace be upon him. Many scholars hold he was the very youth who had carried the basket of fish on Musa's journey to meet al-Khidr, grown now into the man Allah chose to lead the people in. And Mufti Menk notes the quiet mercy in the timing. The forty years were not wasted; they were a changing of the guard. Not one of those who had worshipped the calf and defied Musa marched in Yusha's army. An entire generation had passed, and a new one, raised under the eyes of the pious, born free of the old fear, was the one Allah permitted at last to enter Jerusalem.

Here Mufti Menk tells one of the most arresting signs in the whole series, again from an authentic hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Yusha had laid siege to the city, victory was close, and the sun was setting, which would force the army to break off until morning. So he spoke to the sun. Allah has commanded me, and Allah has commanded you; by His command I tell you to hold. And Allah heard him, and held the sun in its place until the victory was won, and only then let it set. The whole machinery of the sky paused for the prayer of one obedient servant. We ask Allah, Mufti Menk says, for that softness of heart, the kind that still trembles when it hears what its Lord can do.

sections.h2: The people who ran from death

ayah.arabic: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَٰرِهِمْ وَهُمْ أُلُوفٌ حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ فَقَالَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مُوتُوا۟ ثُمَّ أَحْيَٰهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ
ayah.text: Have you not considered those who left their homes in many thousands, fearing death? Allāh said to them, "Die"; then He restored them to life. And Allāh is the possessor of bounty for the people, but most of the people do not show gratitude.

paras: After Yusha, the Children of Israel stayed in that land but never settled into peace: war on the outside against the tyrants around them, and quarrels on the inside among their own twelve tribes. Into that broken time Allah kept sending men. One of them was Hizqeel, whom Mufti Menk introduces with the honesty this whole pillar runs on: whether he was a prophet of Allah or simply a deeply righteous man, the scholars differ, so we say only, peace be upon him, and leave it there. We never harden what revelation left soft.

His story is short and it should stop you cold. A plague broke out, and thousands fled their homes in terror of dying, climbed to the safety of high ground, and settled there sure they had escaped. Then Allah said to them, one word, die. And they all died at once, the very thing they had run from, caught the moment they thought they had outrun it. Allah needs no cause to take a soul; the heart attack and the accident, Mufti Menk says, are mercies, something He gives us to blame so the going is gentle. Here He simply commanded it. And then, the other half of the verse, He restored them to life. They were a sign: you cannot flee the death Allah writes, and you cannot imagine the life He can give back. Most people, the verse ends, will not be grateful for either.

sections.h2: A hundred years, and a meal still warm

ayah.arabic: أَوْ كَٱلَّذِى مَرَّ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا قَالَ أَنَّىٰ يُحْىِۦ هَٰذِهِ ٱللَّهُ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۖ فَأَمَاتَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ ثُمَّ بَعَثَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتَ ۖ قَالَ لَبِثْتُ يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ ۖ قَالَ بَل لَّبِثْتَ مِا۟ئَةَ عَامٍ فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِكَ وَشَرَابِكَ لَمْ يَتَسَنَّهْ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰ حِمَارِكَ وَلِنَجْعَلَكَ ءَايَةً لِّلنَّاسِ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْعِظَامِ كَيْفَ نُنشِزُهَا ثُمَّ نَكْسُوهَا لَحْمًا ۚ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥ قَالَ أَعْلَمُ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
ayah.text: Or [consider such an example] as the one who passed by a township which had fallen into ruin. He said, "How will Allāh bring this to life after its death?" So Allāh caused him to die for a hundred years; then He revived him. He said, "How long have you remained?" He [the man] said, "I have remained a day or part of a day." He said, "Rather, you have remained one hundred years. Look at your food and your drink; it has not changed with time. And look at your donkey; and We will make you a sign for the people. And look at the bones [of this donkey] - how We raise them and then We cover them with flesh." And when it became clear to him, he said, "I know that Allāh is over all things competent."

paras: Then comes the man the Qur'an does not name but the reports often call Uzair, peace be upon him, and again Mufti Menk keeps the door open: prophet or righteous man, the scholars differ. He passed a town fallen to ruin, its roofs caved in, its people long dead, and a question rose in him, not from doubt but from wonder: how will Allah ever bring this back to life? He wanted to see it. So Allah showed him, by making him the demonstration. Allah caused him to die for a hundred years, then woke him, and asked: how long were you here? A day, he said, or part of a day. A hundred years had passed.

Mufti Menk lingers on the gift hidden inside that, because we feel it every night. A century gone, and it felt like an afternoon nap, the way sleep folds the longest night into the single second your eye finally closes. No science has ever fully explained sleep, or the dream, that a man can wander gardens while the man asleep beside him sweats in a nightmare, and neither can reach into the other's world. These are not small things; they are daily proofs of the One who made them. Then Allah gave Uzair the evidence in his hands. Look at your food and drink, after a hundred years, not gone stale. Now look at your donkey: only bones. And watch. He watched the bones lift and knit and clothe themselves in flesh until the animal stood again, his own donkey, alive. And he said it, the line the whole corridor has been building toward: I know that Allah is over all things competent.

sections.h2: When a miracle is mistaken for a god
paras: What happened next is the warning Mufti Menk will not let pass. When Uzair walked back, a hundred years on, no one knew him; his own children had aged past recognising him, and only one woman beyond a hundred, whose sight Allah restored at his du'a, and a birthmark his children remembered, confirmed who he was. And it is said that the Torah had by then been lost in all the fighting and pulling apart, and that Uzair, who had it by heart, dictated it back to the people from memory.

So a man returns from a hundred years dead, restores the lost scripture, and the miracles cluster on him, and his people make the leap a heart should never make. They began to say Uzair is the son of God. Allah records it in the Qur'an, naming it beside the very same claim made later about Isa, peace be upon him. Mufti Menk draws the line plainly, and reverently, never with scorn: a few signs ran through a man's life, and people turned the servant into a son, the gift into a god. It is a test, he says, the same test that meets every messenger who is given something extraordinary. The miracle was never Uzair's to own; it was Allah's, on loan, to point back to Allah. The lesson reaches all the way to us: when you see the gift, thank the Giver, and never let the wonder of the sign pull your worship away from the One who sent it.

sections.h2: A people who wanted a king

ayah.arabic: وَقَالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ بَعَثَ لَكُمْ طَالُوتَ مَلِكًا ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ عَلَيْنَا وَنَحْنُ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْمُلْكِ مِنْهُ وَلَمْ يُؤْتَ سَعَةً مِّنَ ٱلْمَالِ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَزَادَهُۥ بَسْطَةً فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ وَٱلْجِسْمِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يُؤْتِى مُلْكَهُۥ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ
ayah.text: And their prophet said to them, "Indeed, Allāh has sent to you Saul as a king." They said, "How can he have kingship over us while we are more worthy of kingship than him and he has not been given any measure of wealth?" He said, "Indeed, Allāh has chosen him over you and has increased him abundantly in knowledge and stature. And Allāh gives His sovereignty to whom He wills. And Allāh is all-Encompassing [in favor] and Knowing."

paras: The Children of Israel carried a box, the taboot, the Ark, in which lay fragments of the tablets Musa had been given and other relics; whenever they took it into battle they felt secure, and they won. Then, in a time of deep disobedience, it was lost in a war, and their king of the day, hearing the news, dropped dead on the spot. After him they had no king, and they came to their prophet, the one the reports call Samuel, peace be upon him, with a demand: send us a king, and we will fight in the way of Allah for the land that was taken from us.

The prophet read them exactly. Perhaps, he said, if fighting is written for you, you will not fight, the way your fathers refused Musa. They swore they would, driven from their homes and their children as they had been. So Allah named the king: Talut, the one the Bible calls Saul, a man of Bani Israil though not a prophet. And at once they rejected him, on the two grounds men always reach for. He is not from the noble tribe. And he is not rich. Mufti Menk holds the mirror up: we are no different, we still measure a leader by his wealth and his lineage before we ever weigh his knowledge or his worth. But Allah answered over their heads, in words worth keeping: Allah has chosen him over you and given him an abundance in knowledge and in physical strength, and Allah gives His kingship to whom He wills. Take the lesson, Mufti Menk says: when truth reaches you, do not weigh the messenger by his pocket or his name. Whatever reached you was never meant to miss you, and you will answer for what you did with it.

sections.h2: The river, and how many a small company

ayah.arabic: فَلَمَّا فَصَلَ طَالُوتُ بِٱلْجُنُودِ قَالَ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُبْتَلِيكُم بِنَهَرٍ فَمَن شَرِبَ مِنْهُ فَلَيْسَ مِنِّى وَمَن لَّمْ يَطْعَمْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنِّىٓ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱغْتَرَفَ غُرْفَةًۢ بِيَدِهِۦ ۚ فَشَرِبُوا۟ مِنْهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۚ فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَهُۥ هُوَ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ قَالُوا۟ لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا ٱلْيَوْمَ بِجَالُوتَ وَجُنُودِهِۦ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَٰقُوا۟ ٱللَّهِ كَم مِّن فِئَةٍ قَلِيلَةٍ غَلَبَتْ فِئَةً كَثِيرَةًۢ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ مَعَ ٱلصَّٰبِرِينَ
ayah.text: And when Saul went forth with the soldiers, he said, "Indeed, Allāh will be testing you with a river. So whoever drinks from it is not of me, and whoever does not taste it is indeed of me, excepting one who takes [from it] in the hollow of his hand." But they drank from it, except a [very] few of them. Then when he had crossed it along with those who believed with him, they said, "There is no power for us today against Goliath and his soldiers." But those who were certain that they would meet Allāh said, "How many a small company has overcome a large company by permission of Allāh. And Allāh is with the patient."

paras: Talut marched out, and the reports put his army near eighty thousand. He was too wise to trust a crowd that size, knowing how many among them would break the first command they disliked, so Allah set a test at a river on a hot and thirsty day: do not drink, save a single handful in your palm. It was small. It was simple. And the bulk of them, parched, plunged in and drank their fill, disobeying at the very first instruction. Of eighty thousand, the historians say, some seventy-six thousand failed the river. Only a few thousand crossed.

And even those few faltered when they saw what waited on the far bank: Jalut, Goliath, an enormous man at the head of an enormous army, and the cry went up, we have no power today against Jalut and his soldiers. But not everyone said it. The ones who were certain they would meet Allah answered them with the line Mufti Menk says is their whole history in a sentence: how many a small company has overcome a large company by the permission of Allah, and Allah is with the patient. This, he says, is the law underneath every battle that matters. Numbers do not decide it; conviction and patience do. The one who runs becomes a statistic; the one who holds, by Allah's leave, wins. And he points straight ahead to where this pillar is going, to the day the Prophet ﷺ would stand at Badr with three hundred and thirteen against a thousand, and Allah would hand the few the victory, exactly as He did here. The believers at the river were rehearsing Badr centuries before Badr.

sections.h2: A boy, a sling, and the fall of Jalut

ayah.arabic: فَهَزَمُوهُم بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَتَلَ دَاوُۥدُ جَالُوتَ وَءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَهُۥ مِمَّا يَشَآءُ ۗ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَلَٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
ayah.text: So they defeated them by permission of Allāh, and David killed Goliath, and Allāh gave him the kingship and wisdom [i.e., prophethood] and taught him from that which He willed. And if it were not for Allāh checking [some] people by means of others, the earth would have been corrupted, but Allāh is the possessor of bounty for the worlds.

paras: Before the armies clashed they did what was done in those days: a single combat to set the morale, your champion against ours. Jalut stepped out, sword drawn, scoffing. And from the believers' side came a young shepherd with a stick in one hand and a sling and a few pebbles in the other. He told the king he would go. The whole field laughed, Mufti Menk says, Jalut loudest of all, telling the boy one swing of his sword would take off his head. They tried to fit him with armour and it was too heavy; he took it off. He had killed a lion that came for his sheep, he said, and a bear with his bare hands. They let him go.

This was Dawud, David, peace be upon him. He set a pebble in the sling, said Bismillah, swung, and let it fly straight at Jalut's head. The giant dropped, and within a breath he was dead on the ground, cold, and his whole army broke. Allah seals the scene Himself: so they defeated them by Allah's permission, and Dawud killed Jalut, and Allah gave him kingship and wisdom and taught him what He willed. The boy who had passed the river without drinking, who had obeyed when thousands disobeyed, was the one Allah raised to a throne, and we will follow him there tomorrow. Mufti Menk closes the verse on the line that holds the whole corridor of war together: were it not for Allah repelling one people by means of another, the earth would have gone to ruin, but Allah is full of bounty to all the worlds. The small band, the obedient boy, the falling giant, none of it was the boy's strength. It was Allah, working His will through the few who would not run.

dua.arabic: رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَٰفِرِينَ
dua.translit: Rabbana afrigh alayna sabran wa thabbit aqdamana wansurna alal-qawmil-kafirin
dua.en: Our Lord, pour upon us patience and plant firmly our feet and give us victory over the disbelieving people. (the du'a of the believers before Jalut, Surah al-Baqarah 2:250)

lessons_intro: A corridor of short stories, and one current running through all of them: Allah gives life to the dead and victory to the few, so trust Him over the numbers and over the grave.

lessons.text: Musa walked into death the moment he saw no years could change the destination; a whole people died on the high ground where they fled the plague. If you would not be ready to meet Allah now, when would you be? Prepare for the meeting instead of running from it.

lessons.bold: Belief in the Last Day is not a slogan.
lessons.text: The thousands restored to life, and the man woken after a hundred years to see his own donkey rebuilt bone by bone, were shown to us so that resurrection stops being an idea and becomes something Allah let human eyes actually watch. I know that Allah is over all things competent.

lessons.bold: Thank the Giver, never worship the gift.
lessons.text: When miracles clustered on Uzair, his people made him a son of God. A sign is always on loan from Allah to point back to Allah. When you see the wonder, let it lift your worship to the One who sent it, never away from Him.

lessons.bold: Do not weigh the messenger by his pocket.
lessons.text: They rejected Talut for being neither rich nor nobly born, but Allah had chosen him for knowledge and strength. Whatever truth reaches you was never meant to miss you; judge it by what it is, not by who carries it.

Numbers did not decide the river or the battlefield; obedience and patience did. How many a small company has overcome a large company by Allah's permission. The same law would hand the Prophet ﷺ Badr, three hundred and thirteen against a thousand.

faq.q: Who are all these figures between Musa and Dawud?
faq.a: Mufti Menk bridges the gap with several: Yusha ibn Nun (Joshua), the prophet who led a new generation into the holy land after Musa; Hizqeel and Uzair, of whom the scholars differ over whether each was a prophet or a deeply righteous man, so we say only 'peace be upon him' and leave the question where revelation left it; Talut (Saul), the king Allah appointed; Jalut (Goliath), the tyrant he was sent against; and the young Dawud (David), who felled Jalut and would become both prophet and king. He keeps every uncertain identity uncertain rather than hardening it into fact.

faq.q: Where in the Qur'an are these stories?
faq.a: Nearly all of them sit together in Surah al-Baqarah. The people who fled death are in 2:243; the man Allah caused to die a hundred years and then revived is in 2:259; and the whole account of the prophet, Talut, the test of the river, Jalut, and Dawud striking him down runs through 2:246 to 2:251, including the line 'how many a small company has overcome a large company by permission of Allah'. The episodes of Musa's death and Yusha and the sun come from authentic narrations of the Prophet ﷺ rather than the Qur'an.

faq.q: How does this connect to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ?
faq.a: The believers who crossed the river and faced Jalut said, 'how many a small company has overcome a large company by permission of Allah' (al-Baqarah 2:249), and that is exactly what Allah did for the Prophet ﷺ at Badr: roughly three hundred and thirteen believers against a thousand, and the victory was given to the few. Mufti Menk draws the line directly. The small believing band against Jalut was a rehearsal of Badr centuries in advance, the same law of Allah at work, that conviction and patience, not numbers, decide the battles that matter.

faq.q: Why does so much of this corridor return to resurrection?
faq.a: Because belief in the Last Day, in the resurrection of the dead, is an article of iman, and Allah chose this stretch of history to make it visible rather than abstract. A whole people are made to die and then live (2:243); a man sleeps through a hundred years and watches his donkey rebuilt from bare bones (2:259). The reader who wonders, as Uzair did, 'how will Allah bring this to life?' is shown the answer through other people's eyes and led to the same conclusion: 'I know that Allah is over all things competent.'

faq.q: Did the Children of Israel really call Uzair the son of God?
faq.a: Allah records the claim in the Qur'an, placing it beside the later claim made about Isa, peace be upon him. Mufti Menk explains it without any scorn: a cluster of miracles ran through Uzair's life, including, by some reports, dictating the lost Torah back from memory, and his people made the grave error of turning the servant into a son. He treats it as a test that meets every figure given something extraordinary, and a warning to us never to let the awe of a sign pull our worship away from the One who sent it.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: This corridor between Musa and Dawud can feel like a drawer of odd, half-known names, but it is really one sustained answer to the deepest fear a human carries. Can the dead live again? Watch, says Allah, and He shows you a people restored after they fled death, and a man woken from a hundred years with his food still fresh and his donkey reassembled before his eyes, until even the one who once asked 'how?' can only say, 'I know that Allah is over all things competent.' And then, lest you think Allah's power only reaches the grave, He shows it on the battlefield too: the few who obeyed and held their ground felling a giant's army through a boy with a sling, the very pattern He would one day repeat for His final Prophet ﷺ at Badr.

So let it settle into your own iman, where belief in the resurrection and trust in Allah's help both live. O Allah, the One who gives life to the dead and victory to the few, make us certain of the meeting with You, grateful for every sign and never seduced by it, and steady when we are outnumbered. Pour upon us patience, plant our feet firmly, and gather us among those who obeyed when others ran. Ameen.

related.note: Believing in every prophet is the fourth article of your faith

related.label: Belief in the Last Day
related.note: Uzair, and the man Allah let die a hundred years, then woke

slides:
Day 25 of 29.

The corridor between two giants: Musa has just left, Dawud is about to arrive.

In between, a question your faith hangs on: can Allah give life back to the dead?

When the angel of death came for Musa, he struck him.

So Allah offered him more years, one for every hair beneath his hand on an ox.

And then what? he asked. And then death. In that case, let it come now.

After Musa, a new generation entered the holy land under Yusha (Joshua).

Victory was near but the sun was setting. So he spoke to the sun.

And Allah held it in place until the battle was won.

A plague came. Thousands fled their homes in terror of dying, sure they had escaped to high ground.

Then Allah said one word: die.

Then He brought them back to life. You cannot outrun the death He writes. (al-Baqarah 2:243)

A man asked: how will Allah bring this ruined town back to life?

So Allah made him the answer. He died for a hundred years, then woke thinking it was an afternoon.

His food: still fresh. His donkey: rebuilt from bones, before his eyes. (al-Baqarah 2:259)

They wanted a king. Allah sent Talut.

They rejected him: not rich enough, not nobly born.

But Allah chose him for knowledge and strength. Never weigh the messenger by his pocket. (al-Baqarah 2:247)

At the river, eighty thousand were told: do not drink, just one handful.

Most drank. Only a few thousand crossed.

Facing a giant's army, the believers said: how many a small company has overcome a large one, by Allah's permission. (al-Baqarah 2:249)

A shepherd boy with a sling stepped out against Jalut. The whole field laughed.

He said Bismillah, and one pebble dropped the giant dead.

This was Dawud. The same law would give the Prophet ﷺ Badr: 313 against a thousand. #StoriesOfTheProphets
social.caption: Day 25 of 29: between Musa and Dawud. A corridor of short, half-known stories, all circling one question your iman rests on: can Allah give life back to the dead? He shows you a people restored after they fled a plague (al-Baqarah 2:243), and a man woken after a hundred years to find his food still fresh and his donkey rebuilt bone by bone (2:259), until even the one who asked 'how?' can only say, 'I know that Allah is over all things competent.' Then He proves His power on the battlefield: Talut's small obedient band breaking Jalut's army through a shepherd boy named Dawud, who said Bismillah and dropped a giant with one stone (2:246-251). 'How many a small company has overcome a large company by permission of Allah', the very pattern Allah would repeat for the Prophet ﷺ at Badr, 313 against a thousand. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved series (his full lecture is embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A prophet a day, connected back to the Qur'an and the life of the Prophet ﷺ, at buruja.com. #StoriesOfTheProphets #MuftiMenk #Dawud #Talut #Quran #alBaqarah #Badr #iman #Buruja #islam

email.subject: He slept a hundred years and thought it was an afternoon
email.alt_subject: A boy, a sling, and the fall of a giant
email.preview: Day 25 of 29: the corridor between Musa and Dawud, and the one question it keeps answering.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

Most days in the Stories of the Prophets are one life, told slowly. Today is a corridor, the stretch between two giants, Musa who has just left us and Dawud who is about to arrive, and Mufti Menk walks us down it past a handful of figures most of us have never had named for us.

It begins with Musa's own death. When the angel of death came for him, he struck him, and Allah offered him more years, one for every hair his hand could cover on the back of an ox. And then what, Musa asked. And then death. In that case, he said, let it come now. No number of years changes the destination. If you would not be ready to meet Allah now, when exactly would you be?

After him, a new generation entered the holy land under Yusha (Joshua), peace be upon him, and when the setting sun threatened to end the battle before the victory, he spoke to the sun, and Allah held it still until the work was done.

Then the corridor turns to its real subject, which is the deepest fear a human carries: can the dead live again? Watch, says Allah. A plague came, and thousands fled their homes in terror of dying, climbed to safety, and Allah said one word, die, the very thing they had run from. Then He brought them back to life (Surah al-Baqarah 2:243). And a man passed a town in ruins and wondered how Allah could ever revive it, so Allah made him the demonstration: He caused him to die for a hundred years, then woke him thinking he had napped an afternoon, his food still fresh, his donkey rebuilt from bare bones before his eyes (2:259). All the man could say was, 'I know that Allah is over all things competent.' That is what belief in the Last Day is meant to feel like, not a slogan, but something Allah let human eyes actually watch.

Then He proves the same power on the battlefield. The people asked for a king, and Allah sent Talut, whom they rejected for being neither rich nor nobly born (2:247). At a river he tested eighty thousand with a single command, do not drink more than a handful, and most failed it; only a few thousand crossed. Facing Jalut's enormous army, the believers who were certain of meeting Allah said the line that is their whole history: how many a small company has overcome a large company by the permission of Allah (2:249). And the giant fell, not to an army, but to a shepherd boy with a sling who said Bismillah and let one stone fly. This was Dawud, peace be upon him (2:251).

Mufti Menk draws the line straight to where this series is heading: that same law of Allah, the few given victory over the many, is exactly what He would do for the Prophet ﷺ at Badr, three hundred and thirteen against a thousand. The believers at the river were rehearsing Badr centuries in advance.

Tonight, one du'a, the one they made before the giant: Our Lord, pour upon us patience, plant our feet firmly, and give us victory.

Tomorrow we follow that boy to his throne. Read day 25, and watch Mufti Menk's full lecture, at the link below.
